Title: The Startup Dev Cold Outreach System 2026 | 5 Steps From Cold to Client
Description: Save this for your freelance dev client acquisition and cold outreach strategy board. Most freelance developers send cold emails that read like cover letters — generic, feature-focused, self-centred. Startup founders delete them in 3 seconds. This 5-step outreach system flips the script: every touchpoint is built around the founder's specific pain point, not your portfolio. The response rate difference is not marginal. Save this before you send another cold pitch. #FreelanceDev #ClientAcquisition #ColdOutreach #SoloFounder #StartupLife #TechConsulting #BuildInPublic
Alt Text: Dark editorial infographic showing 5-step freelance dev cold outreach system, each step in amber-numbered sequence with founder pain point framing, deep charcoal background, do/don't contrast column, clean Outfit typography, no photography
AI Prompt: Clean editorial infographic, 9:16 vertical, Style B Dynamic Viral. Background: deep charcoal (#0d0d1a). Title bold white: THE STARTUP DEV COLD OUTREACH SYSTEM — 2026. Subtitle amber: Stop sending cover letters. Start solving problems. Below, 5 steps in amber-numbered sequence: STEP 1 — RESEARCH FIRST (10 min): Find one specific, public pain point (recent tweet, product review, job listing gap). Your email must reference this. STEP 2 — SUBJECT LINE = THEIR PROBLEM: Not 'Experienced React Developer Available.' Try: 'Your checkout drop-off — I think I know why.' STEP 3 — 3 PARAGRAPHS MAXIMUM: Para 1: Their problem (1 sentence). Para 2: Your relevant proof (1-2 sentences). Para 3: Single ask (15-min call, not a portfolio link). STEP 4 — FOLLOW UP ONCE (day 5): One short follow-up. Subject: 'Still relevant?' Body: one sentence. STEP 5 — TRACK IN A SIMPLE CRM: Notion or Airtable. 10 prospects active at all times. Two columns contrast below: WHAT FOUNDERS DELETE IMMEDIATELY (generic intro, portfolio link, rate mention) vs WHAT FOUNDERS ACTUALLY REPLY TO (specific problem reference, short ask, proof of understanding). Footer amber: 10 targeted pitches beat 100 generic ones every time. Colour: charcoal, amber, white. No photography.

Description: Save this for your SaaS tech stack and startup founder resources board. Most founders spend 3 months debating tech stack before building anything. This is the 2026 opinionated SaaS MVP stack — the exact combination of tools that lets a solo dev or small team ship a full SaaS product in 6 weeks or less, without lock-in, without over-engineering, and without a devops team. If you're a founder evaluating a dev, ask them if they know this stack. If you're a dev building for founders, memorise it. Save this. #SaaSStack #StartupDev #MVP #FreelanceDev #TechStack #SoloFounder #BuildInPublic
Alt Text: Dark editorial tech infographic showing 2026 SaaS MVP stack in 6 horizontal layers (Frontend / Backend / Database / Auth / Payments / Infra), each layer showing recommended tool with brief rationale, deep charcoal background with teal-amber colour coding, clean Outfit typography
AI Prompt: Clean editorial infographic, 9:16 vertical, Style B Dynamic Viral. Background: deep charcoal. Title bold white: THE 2026 SAAS MVP STACK. Subtitle amber: Ship in 6 weeks. No devops team required. Below, 6 horizontal layer cards stacked vertically: LAYER 1 — FRONTEND: Next.js 15 + Tailwind CSS. Rationale: SSR by default, Vercel deploy in minutes, massive ecosystem. LAYER 2 — BACKEND/API: Next.js API routes + tRPC. Rationale: Type-safe end-to-end, no separate API server to manage. LAYER 3 — DATABASE: Supabase (Postgres). Rationale: Managed Postgres with realtime + row-level security built in. LAYER 4 — AUTH: Clerk or Supabase Auth. Rationale: Social login, magic link, MFA in an afternoon. Not a distraction. LAYER 5 — PAYMENTS: Stripe Billing. Rationale: Subscriptions, metered billing, webhooks. The standard for reason. LAYER 6 — INFRA / DEPLOY: Vercel (frontend) + Railway (backend services). Rationale: Zero devops for first 1000 users. Bottom panel amber: TOTAL MONTHLY COST AT ZERO REVENUE: ~$0–$25. This stack scales to $1M ARR without a platform change. Footer: Stack decisions are not permanent. Speed is. Colour: charcoal, teal, amber, white. No photography.

Subtitle amber: Always have 10 in motion. Never scramble again. Below, 5 pipeline stages shown as a funnel narrowing from top to bottom: STAGE 1 — IDENTIFIED (10 prospects): Criteria: specific startup type, specific pain point match. Time in stage: 1 day. STAGE 2 — RESEARCHED (10 prospects → 7 move forward): Research: 1 specific problem to reference. Conversion gate: 'Can I write 3 sentences about their problem?' STAGE 3 — CONTACTED (7 → 4): Outreach: personalised 3-paragraph email. Follow-up: once on day 5. Conversion gate: reply or call booked. STAGE 5 — CLOSED (2 → 1 deal): Contract signed. Onboarding triggered. New cycle begins immediately. Bottom panel: PIPELINE MATH: 10 prospects identified → 1 closed deal per 3-week cycle → 12K–25K/month at steady state. Critical amber note: Most freelancers skip Stage 2. Title: What Founders Really Look For in a Dev 2026 | The 6-Point Internal Checklist
Description: Save this for your freelance developer positioning and founder psychology board. Most developers pitch their technical skills. Founders are not buying technical skills — they're buying certainty, trust, and business judgment. This is the actual 6-point internal checklist founders use when evaluating a developer, extracted from founder interviews and hiring post-mortems. Every point on this list is a positioning opportunity for a freelance dev who understands it. Save this before your next pitch. #FreelanceDev #FounderPsychology #DevPositioning #TechConsulting #ClientAcquisition #SoloFounder #BuildInPublic
Alt Text: Dark editorial infographic showing 6-point founder internal checklist for evaluating developers, each point with founder internal monologue in quotes and freelance dev positioning implication, deep charcoal background with amber-teal colour coding, clean professional Outfit typography
AI Prompt: Clean editorial infographic, 9:16 vertical, Style B Dynamic Viral. Background: deep charcoal. Title bold white: WHAT FOUNDERS REALLY LOOK FOR IN A DEV. Subtitle amber: It's not your technical skills. It's your judgment. Below, 6 numbered checklist items, each with two parts — FOUNDER'S INTERNAL QUESTION (in italic quote style, amber) and DEV POSITIONING IMPLICATION (white): 1. 'Do they understand my business, not just my codebase?' → Position: Lead with the business problem. Show you read their metrics, not just their job post. 2. 'Have they actually shipped something?' → Position: Your portfolio is outcomes, not features. Show what happened after you shipped. 3. 'Will they tell me when I'm wrong?' → Position: In the proposal call, respectfully challenge one assumption. Founders hire people who push back. 4. 'Can I explain this to my investors?' → Position: Write and speak in business language. Avoid jargon in all written communication. 5. 'Are they going to disappear?' → Position: Offer a structured engagement: defined check-ins, async updates, monthly report. Stability signals. 6. 'Can I trust them with my worst problems?' → Position: In your first call, ask about their biggest unsolved technical challenge. Then listen. Bottom panel amber: Technical skills are table stakes. Trust is the product. Colour: charcoal, amber, teal, white. No photography.
